title: Distributions of Class Groups of Global Fields
speaker: Melanie Matchett Wood
abstract: Cohen, Lenstra, and Martinet have given highly influential conjectures on the distribution of class groups of number fields, the finite abelian groups that control the factorization in number fields. Malle, using tabulation of class groups of number fields, found that the Cohen-Lenstra-Martinet heuristics for the distributions of class groups of extensions of a number field seemed incorrect when the base field contains roots of unity.
tags: Visions in Arithmetic and Beyond: Celebrating Peter Sarnak's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Cohen–Lenstra–Martinet framework for reduced G-module class groups
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the arithmetic-statistics problem for class groups in families of number fields, replaces the class group by the invariant-free reduced class group as a finite G-module, and states the Cohen–Lenstra–Martinet prediction with automorphism and signature factors.

proof_stages.label: Roots-of-unity failures of the classical heuristics
proof_stages.summary: Malle’s computations and subsequent work are presented as evidence that the Cohen–Lenstra–Martinet distributions fail for p-primary class groups when the base field contains p-power roots of unity, with only partial earlier corrections available.

proof_stages.label: Function-field moments and the inverse moment input
proof_stages.summary: A theorem in the function-field setting, proved using moduli of curves over finite fields, supplies exact limiting surjection moments, and a moment-problem theorem of the speaker and Sawin shows that these moments determine a unique explicit G-module distribution.

proof_stages.label: General conjecture stated by surjection moments
proof_stages.summary: For p not dividing |G| and a base field containing exactly p^r-th roots of unity, the proposed number-field distribution is characterized by expected numbers of G-module surjections to every finite invariant-free module B, with the moment involving |B| and the G-invariants in ∧²B.

proof_stages.label: Explicit distributions and zero-probability module types
proof_stages.summary: The inverse-moment machinery turns the function-field moments into explicit probability formulas; these formulas retain automorphism factors but also couple dual irreducible representations and sometimes assign probability zero to G-modules.

proof_stages.label: Nonexistence theorem for excessive dual-rank imbalance
proof_stages.summary: The zeros in the distribution are explained by a number-field theorem bounding, for each pair of distinct dual irreducible mod-p representations of G, the differences of the p^l-ranks of the corresponding class-group summands; the theorem proves all zero cases predicted by the conjecture.

proof_stages.label: Reflection-principle mechanism behind the rank bounds
proof_stages.summary: The rank restrictions are traced to a generalized reflection principle: class field theory identifies a maximal unramified p^r-torsion Galois group with the class group modulo p^r, while Kummer theory pairs it with virtual p^r-powers close to p^r-torsion in the class group, yielding controlled inequalities rather than exact equalities.

proof_stages.label: Scope, sharpness, and clarifications
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion and Q&A emphasize that a probabilistic function-field theorem leads to number-field nonexistence results and explicit positive conjectural probabilities, while clarifying the q→∞ limiting process, the rational-function-field hypothesis, choices of infinite places, and possible extensions involving added pseudo-infinite primes and nonabelian structures.

examples.name: Z/9 versus Z/3 × Z/3 in quadratic class groups
examples.note: The comparison of two same-size 3-groups in computed quadratic class groups motivates why a distribution should weight groups by automorphisms rather than merely by size.
examples.engagement_reason: The groups are cited as a motivating contrast in Cohen–Lenstra tables, with no computation of their automorphisms or probabilities carried out.

passes.reason: Ordinary finite abelian 3-groups of order 9 used to rule out a size-only explanation of class-group frequencies.
the elementary-abelian option has extra automorphism symmetry, and the same-order frequency gap rules out a size-only heuristic.

They are ordinary finite abelian 3-groups of the same order, used to motivate automorphism weighting rather than to refute a claim.

The comparison motivates automorphism-weighting in standard order-9 abelian 3-groups, with Z/3×Z/3 the highly symmetric vector-space case rather than a refuting example.

examples.name: Real versus imaginary quadratic fields
examples.note: The familiar difference between real and imaginary quadratic class groups illustrates why signature and complex-conjugation factors enter Cohen–Lenstra–Martinet distributions.
examples.engagement_reason: Real and imaginary quadratic fields are contrasted for signature-dependent behavior, but the talk does not work through a concrete quadratic-field calculation.

examples.name: Malle’s 2-rank data for cyclic cubic fields
examples.note: The cyclic cubic field tables show a visible failure of the Cohen–Lenstra–Martinet prediction at p=2 and lead to Malle’s corrected empirical formula.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes Malle’s cyclic cubic 2-rank data, explains the even-rank representation constraint, and compares explicit Cohen–Lenstra and Malle formulas/statistics.

passes.reason: Cyclic cubic fields are Galois degree-3 extensions, and Malle’s 2-rank data empirically contradicts the CLM p=2 prediction.

examples.name: Quadratic extensions of Q(ζ3)
examples.note: The failure of the predicted 3-rank distribution over a base field containing third roots of unity illustrates the broader roots-of-unity obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: Quadratic extensions of Q(ζ3) are given as an empirical case where 3-ranks disagree with the old prediction, but no formula or construction is worked through.

examples.name: C7-extensions and the p=2 formula
examples.note: The explicit cyclic degree-seven, p=2 distribution displays zero-probability rank patterns, prompting the nonexistence/rank-inequality theorem for dual representations.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to G=C7 and p=2, describes the two irreducible representations and ranks, presents the explicit probability formula, and interprets its zero cases.

passes.reason: C7-extensions form a cyclic Galois family, and the p=2 formula gives zero-probability modules against the old all-positive heuristic.

examples.name: Scholz reflection via a biquadratic field
examples.note: Recasting the classical relation between the 3-ranks of Q(√d) and Q(√−3d) inside the biquadratic field Q(√d,√−3) reveals the general viewpoint of reflection as a restriction within one G-module class group.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker recasts Scholz reflection using the biquadratic field, identifies quadratic class groups with irreducible-representation pieces, and reasons through the resulting rank restriction.

passes.reason: The biquadratic field is a maximally Galois quartic field used to recast Scholz reflection as an obstruction to arbitrary independent ranks.
the biquadratic V4 Galois field is structurally symmetric and is used to reinterpret Scholz reflection rather than as an adversarial example.
